questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.questionText: The integral $\int\limits_0^1 \cot ^{-1}\left(1+x+x^2\right) d x$ is equal to :
questions.solution:
Let

$$ I=\int_0^1 \cot^{-1}(1+x+x^2)\,dx $$

We will use the standard identity

$$ \cot^{-1} t=\tan^{-1}\left(\frac{1}{t}\right)\quad \text{for } t>0 $$

Since $1+x+x^2>0$ on $[0,1]$, we can write

$$ I=\int_0^1 \tan^{-1}\left(\frac{1}{1+x+x^2}\right)\,dx $$

Now use the formula

$$ \tan^{-1} a-\tan^{-1} b=\tan^{-1}\left(\frac{a-b}{1+ab}\right) $$

We try to express

$$ \frac{1}{1+x+x^2} $$

in a suitable form. Observe that

$$ \tan^{-1}(x+1)-\tan^{-1}x = \tan^{-1}\left(\frac{(x+1)-x}{1+x(x+1)}\right) = \tan^{-1}\left(\frac{1}{1+x+x^2}\right) $$

Hence,

$$ \cot^{-1}(1+x+x^2)=\tan^{-1}(x+1)-\tan^{-1}x $$

So,

$$ I=\int_0^1 \left[\tan^{-1}(x+1)-\tan^{-1}x\right]dx $$

Therefore,

$$ I=\int_0^1 \tan^{-1}(x+1)\,dx-\int_0^1 \tan^{-1}x\,dx $$

In the first integral, put $u=x+1$. Then when $x=0$, $u=1$, and when $x=1$, $u=2$. So

$$ \int_0^1 \tan^{-1}(x+1)\,dx=\int_1^2 \tan^{-1}u\,du $$

Thus,

$$ I=\int_1^2 \tan^{-1}u\,du-\int_0^1 \tan^{-1}x\,dx = \int_1^2 \tan^{-1}x\,dx-\int_0^1 \tan^{-1}x\,dx $$

Now use

$$ \int \tan^{-1}x\,dx=x\tan^{-1}x-\frac{1}{2}\log(1+x^2) $$

So,

$$ \int_1^2 \tan^{-1}x\,dx = \left[x\tan^{-1}x-\frac{1}{2}\log(1+x^2)\right]_1^2 $$

and

$$ \int_0^1 \tan^{-1}x\,dx = \left[x\tan^{-1}x-\frac{1}{2}\log(1+x^2)\right]_0^1 $$

Hence,

$$ I= \left[x\tan^{-1}x-\frac{1}{2}\log(1+x^2)\right]_1^2 - \left[x\tan^{-1}x-\frac{1}{2}\log(1+x^2)\right]_0^1 $$

This becomes

$$ I= \left(2\tan^{-1}2-\frac{1}{2}\log 5\right) - \left(\tan^{-1}1-\frac{1}{2}\log 2\right) -\left(\tan^{-1}1-\frac{1}{2}\log 2\right) $$

since at $x=0$, the value is $0$.

Now $\tan^{-1}1=\dfrac{\pi}{4}$, so

$$ I=2\tan^{-1}2-\frac{1}{2}\log 5-2\left(\frac{\pi}{4}-\frac{1}{2}\log 2\right) $$

$$ I=2\tan^{-1}2-\frac{1}{2}\log 5-\frac{\pi}{2}+\log 2 $$

Now combine the logarithms:

$$ \log 2-\frac{1}{2}\log 5 = \frac{1}{2}\log 4-\frac{1}{2}\log 5 = \frac{1}{2}\log\left(\frac{4}{5}\right) = -\frac{1}{2}\log\left(\frac{5}{4}\right) $$

Therefore,

$$ I=2\tan^{-1}2-\frac{1}{2}\log\left(\frac{5}{4}\right)-\frac{\pi}{2} $$

So the correct answer is

$$ \boxed{2\tan^{-1}2-\frac{1}{2}\log_e\left(\frac{5}{4}\right)-\frac{\pi}{2}} $$

Hence, the correct option is:

$$ \boxed{\text{Option D}} $$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.questionText: Let $$p(x)$$ be a function defined on $$R$$ such that $$p'(x)=p'(1-x),$$ for all $$x \in \left[ {0,1} \right],p\left( 0 \right) = 1$$ and $$p(1)=41.$$ Then $$\int\limits_0^1 {p\left( x \right)dx} $$ equals :
questions.solution:
$$p'\left( x \right) = p'\left( {1 - x} \right)$$

$$ \Rightarrow p\left( x \right) = - p\left( {1 - x} \right) + c$$

at $$x=0$$

$$p\left( 0 \right) = - p\left( 1 \right) + c \Rightarrow 42 = c$$

Now, $$p\left( x \right) = - p\left( {1 - x} \right) + 42$$

$$ \Rightarrow p\left( x \right) + p\left( {1 - x} \right) = 42$$

$$ \Rightarrow I = \int\limits_0^1 {p\left( x \right)dx\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,...\left( i \right)} $$

$$ \Rightarrow I = \int\limits_0^1 {p\left( {1 - x} \right)} dx\,\,\,\,\,\,...\left( {ii} \right)$$

on adding $$(i)$$ and $$(ii),$$

$$\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,$$$$2I = \int\limits_0^1 {\left( {42} \right)dx \Rightarrow I = 21} $$

questions.solution:
Let $$I = \int\limits_{ - 1}^1 {\log \left( {x + \sqrt {{x^2} + 1} } \right)dx} $$

$$\because$$ $$\log \left( {x + \sqrt {{x^2} + 1} } \right)$$ is an odd function

$$\therefore$$ I = 0

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.questionText:
Let $$f: \mathbb{R} \rightarrow \mathbb{R}$$ be a function defined as

$$f(x)=a \sin \left(\frac{\pi[x]}{2}\right)+[2-x], a \in \mathbb{R}$$ where $$[t]$$ is the greatest integer less than or equal to $$t$$. If $$\mathop {\lim }\limits_{x \to -1 } f(x)$$ exists, then the value of $$\int\limits_{0}^{4} f(x) d x$$ is equal to
questions.solution:
$$f(x) = a\sin \left( {{{\pi [x]} \over 2}} \right) + [2 - x]\,a \in R$$

Now,

$$\because$$ $$\mathop {\lim }\limits_{x \to - 1} f(x)$$ exist

$$\therefore$$ $$\mathop {\lim }\limits_{x \to - {1^ - }} f(x) = \mathop {\lim }\limits_{x \to - {1^ + }} f(x)$$

$$ \Rightarrow a\sin \left( {{{ - 2\pi } \over 2}} \right) + 3 = a\sin \left( {{{ - \pi } \over 2}} \right) + 2$$

$$ \Rightarrow - a = 1 \Rightarrow a = - 1$$

Now, $$\int_0^4 {f(x)dx = \int_0^4 {\left( { - \sin \left( {{{\pi [x]} \over 2}} \right) + [2 - x]} \right)dx} } $$

$$ = \int_0^1 {1dx + \int_1^2 { - 1dx + \int_2^3 { - 1dx + \int_3^4 {(1 - 2)dx} } } } $$

$$ = 1 - 1 - 1 - 1 = - 2$$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.solution:
$$\mathop {\lim }\limits_{n \to \alpha } \sum\limits_{r = 1}^n {{r \over {2{r^2} - 7rn + 6{n^2}}}} $$

$$ = \mathop {\lim }\limits_{n \to \alpha } {1 \over n}\sum\limits_{r = 1}^n {{{\left( {{r \over n}} \right)} \over {2{{\left( {{r \over n}} \right)}^2} - 7\left( {{r \over n}} \right) + 6}}} $$

$$ = \int_a^b {f(x)dx} $$

$$a = \mathop {\lim }\limits_{n \to \alpha } \left( {{1 \over n}} \right) = 0$$

$$b = \mathop {\lim }\limits_{n \to \alpha } \left( {{n \over n}} \right) = 1$$

and $${r \over n} \to x$$

$$ = \int_0^1 {{x \over {2{x^2} - 7x + 6}}dx} $$

$$ = \int_0^1 {{x \over {2{x^2} - 3x - 4x + 6}}dx} $$

$$ = \int_0^1 {{x \over {(2x - 3)(x - 2)}}dx} $$

$$ = \int_0^1 {\left[ {{A \over {(2x - 3)}} + {B \over {(x - 2)}}} \right]dx} $$

$$ = \int_0^1 {\left( {{{ - 3} \over {2x - 3}} + {2 \over {x - 2}}} \right)dx} $$

$$ = \left[ { - {{3\log |2x - 3|} \over 2} + 2\log |x - 2|} \right]_0^1$$

$$ = - {3 \over 2}\left[ {\log ( - 1) - \log ( - 3)} \right] + 2\left[ {\log ( - 1) - \log ( - 2)} \right]$$

$$ = - {3 \over 2}\log \left( {{1 \over 3}} \right) + 2\log \left( {{1 \over 2}} \right)$$

$$ = + {3 \over 2}\log 3 - 2\log 2$$

$$ = \log \sqrt {{3^3}} - \log 4$$

$$ = \log {{\sqrt {{3^2} \times 3} } \over 4}$$

$$ = \log {{3\sqrt 3 } \over 4}$$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.solution:
Answer: $\ln(8\sqrt{e})$

Detailed Reasoning:

We are given a functional equation and need to find $ 8 \int_1^2 f(x) \, dx $.

Step-by-Step Solution:

Given Functional Equation:

$ 3f(x) + f\left(\frac{1}{x}\right) = 1 + \frac{1}{x}. $

Replace $ x $ by $ \frac{1}{x} $:

$ 3f\left(\frac{1}{x}\right) + f(x) = 1 + x. $

System of Linear Equations:

We now have a system of two equations:

$ 3f(x) + f\left(\frac{1}{x}\right) = 1 + \frac{1}{x}, \tag{1} $

$ f(x) + 3f\left(\frac{1}{x}\right) = 1 + x. \tag{2} $

Consider these as linear equations in terms of $ f(x) $ and $ f(1/x) $.

Solving the System:

From equation (1):

$ 3f(x) + f\left(\frac{1}{x}\right) = 1 + \frac{1}{x}. $

From equation (2):

$ f(x) + 3f\left(\frac{1}{x}\right) = 1 + x. $

Let's solve for $ f(x) $. Multiply (1) by 3:

$ 9f(x) + 3f\left(\frac{1}{x}\right) = 3 + \frac{3}{x}. $

Subtract (2) from this:

$ (9f(x) + 3f(1/x)) - (f(x) + 3f(1/x)) = \left(3 + \frac{3}{x}\right) - (1 + x). $

On the left side, the $ f(1/x) $ terms cancel out:

$ 8f(x) = (3 + \frac{3}{x}) - (1 + x). $

Simplify the right side:

$ 3 + \frac{3}{x} - 1 - x = 2 + \frac{3}{x} - x = \frac{2x + 3 - x^2}{x}. $

Thus:

$ 8f(x) = \frac{2x + 3 - x^2}{x} \implies f(x) = \frac{2x + 3 - x^2}{8x}. $

Rewrite $ f(x) $:

$ f(x) = \frac{-x^2 + 2x + 3}{8x}. $

Integrating $ f(x) $ from 1 to 2:

Split the fraction:

$ f(x) = \frac{-x^2}{8x} + \frac{2x}{8x} + \frac{3}{8x} = -\frac{x}{8} + \frac{1}{4} + \frac{3}{8x}. $

Therefore:

$ \int_1^2 f(x) \, dx = \int_1^2 \left(-\frac{x}{8} + \frac{1}{4} + \frac{3}{8x}\right) dx. $

Integrate term by term:

$\int_1^2 -\frac{x}{8} dx = -\frac{1}{8} \cdot \frac{x^2}{2}\Big|_1^2 = -\frac{1}{16}(4 - 1) = -\frac{3}{16}.$

$\int_1^2 \frac{1}{4} dx = \frac{1}{4}(2 - 1) = \frac{1}{4}.$

$\int_1^2 \frac{3}{8x} dx = \frac{3}{8}\ln(x)\Big|_1^2 = \frac{3}{8}\ln(2).$

Sum these results:

$ \int_1^2 f(x) dx = \left(-\frac{3}{16} + \frac{1}{4} + \frac{3}{8}\ln(2)\right). $

Combine the fractions $-\frac{3}{16}$ and $\frac{1}{4} = \frac{4}{16}$:

$ \int_1^2 f(x) dx = \frac{4}{16} - \frac{3}{16} + \frac{3}{8}\ln(2) = \frac{1}{16} + \frac{3}{8}\ln(2). $

Multiplying by 8:

We need $ 8 \int_1^2 f(x) dx $:

$ 8 \left(\frac{1}{16} + \frac{3}{8}\ln(2)\right) = 8 \cdot \frac{1}{16} + 8 \cdot \frac{3}{8}\ln(2) = \frac{1}{2} + 3\ln(2). $

Matching to Given Options:

Check option A: $\ln(8\sqrt{e})$

$ \ln(8\sqrt{e}) = \ln(8) + \ln(e^{1/2}) = \ln(8) + \frac{1}{2} = 3\ln(2) + \frac{1}{2}. $

This exactly matches our result $ \frac{1}{2} + 3\ln(2) $.

Final Answer:

$ 8\int_1^2 f(x)dx = \ln(8\sqrt{e}) $

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.questionText:
Let $$F:[3,5] \to R$$ be a twice differentiable function on (3, 5) such that

$$F(x) = {e^{ - x}}\int\limits_3^x {(3{t^2} + 2t + 4F'(t))dt} $$. If $$F'(4) = {{\alpha {e^\beta } - 224} \over {{{({e^\beta } - 4)}^2}}}$$, then $$\alpha$$ + $$\beta$$ is equal to _______________.

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.solution:
$$\mathop {\lim }\limits_{x \to {0^ + }} {{\int\limits_0^{{x^2}} {\sin (\sqrt t )dt} } \over {{x^3}}}$$

This is in $${0 \over 0}$$ form, so use L' Hospital rule

$$ = \mathop {\lim }\limits_{x \to {0^ + }} {{{d \over {dx}}\left( {\int\limits_0^{{x^2}} {\sin (\sqrt t )dt} } \right)} \over {{d \over {dx}}\left( {{x^3}} \right)}}$$

$$ = \mathop {\lim }\limits_{x \to {0^ + }} {{\sin x.2x - 0} \over {3{x^2}}}$$

(applying Leibnitz rule)

$$ = {2 \over 3}\mathop {\lim }\limits_{x \to {0^ + }} {{\sin x} \over x}$$

$$ = {2 \over 3}$$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.solution:
$\int\limits_0^1 \underbrace{4 x^3}_{\mathrm{I}} \underbrace{\frac{d^2}{d x^2}\left(1-x^2\right)^5}_{\mathrm{II}} d x$

Integrating by parts:

$$ I=4 x^3\left[\frac{d}{d x}\left(1-x^2\right)^5\right]_0^1-\int\limits_0^1 12 x^2 \frac{d}{d x}\left(1-x^2\right)^5 d x $$

$\begin{aligned} & =4 x^3\left[5\left(1-x^2\right)^4(-2 x)\right]_0^1-12\left[\left[x^2\left(1-x^2\right)^5\right]_0^1-\int\limits_0^1 2 x\left(1-x^2\right)^5 d x\right. \\\\ & =0-0+12 \int\limits_0^1 2 x\left(1-x^2\right)^5 d x\end{aligned}$

Now, putting $1-x^2=t$, we get $-2 x d r=d t$. Therefore,

$$ I=-12 \int\limits_1^0 t^5 d t $$

$\begin{aligned} & \text { When } x=0, t=1 . \\\\ & \text { When } x=1, t=0 .\end{aligned}$

$I=12 \times \int\limits_0^1 t^5 d t=12 \times\left[\frac{t^6}{6}\right]_0^1=12 \times \frac{1}{6}=2$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.solution:
$$I = \int\limits_0^{10\pi } {\left| {\sin x} \right|} dx$$

$$ = 10\int\limits_0^\pi {\left| {\sin x\,} \right|} \,dx$$

$$ = 10\int\limits_0^\pi {\sin \,x\,dx} $$

$$\left[ \, \right.$$ as $$\left| {\sin x} \right|$$ is periodic with period $$\pi $$

and $$sin$$ $$x > 0$$ if $$0 < x$$ $$ < \pi $$ $$\left. \, \right]$$

$$I = 20\int\limits_0^{\pi /2} {\sin x} \,dx$$

$$ = 20\left[ { - \cos \,x_0^{\pi /2}} \right] = 20$$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.solution:
We have, $\alpha(m, n)=\int\limits_0^2 t^m(1+3 t)^n d t$

Also, $11 \alpha(10,6)+18 \alpha(11,5)=P(14)^6$

$\Rightarrow 11 \int\limits_0^2 t^{10}(1+3 t)^6 d t+18 \int\limits_0^2 t^{11}(1+3 t)^5 d t=P(14)^6$

Using integration by part for expression $t^{10}(1+3 t)^6$

$$ \begin{array}{r} \left.\Rightarrow 11\left[(1+3 t)^6 \times \frac{t^{11}}{11}\right]_0^2-\int\limits_0^2 6(1+3 t)^5 \times 3 \times \frac{t^{11}}{11} d t\right] \\\\ +18 \int\limits_0^2 t^{11}(1+3 t)^5 d t=P(14)^6 \end{array} $$

$$ \begin{aligned} & \Rightarrow 7^6 \times 2^{11}-18 \int\limits_0^2 t^{11}(1+3 t)^5 d t+18 \int\limits_0^2 t^{11}(1+3 t)^5 d t=P(14)^6 \\\\ & \Rightarrow 7^6 \times 2^{11}=P(14)^6 \Rightarrow(7 \times 2)^6 \times 2^5=P(14)^6 \\\\ & \Rightarrow P=2^5=32 \end{aligned} $$

questions.questionText: If   $$\int\limits_0^{{\pi  \over 3}} {{{\tan \theta } \over {\sqrt {2k\,\sec \theta } }}} \,d\theta  = 1 - {1 \over {\sqrt 2 }},\left( {k > 0} \right),$$ then value of k is :

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.questionText: Let $$y=f(x)$$ be a thrice differentiable function in $$(-5,5)$$. Let the tangents to the curve $$y=f(x)$$ at $$(1, f(1))$$ and $$(3, f(3))$$ make angles $$\pi / 6$$ and $$\pi / 4$$, respectively with positive $$x$$-axis. If $$27 \int_\limits1^3\left(\left(f^{\prime}(t)\right)^2+1\right) f^{\prime \prime}(t) d t=\alpha+\beta \sqrt{3}$$ where $$\alpha, \beta$$ are integers, then the value of $$\alpha+\beta$$ equals
questions.solution:
$$\begin{aligned} & y=f(x) \Rightarrow \frac{d y}{d x}=f^{\prime}(x) \\ & \left.\frac{d y}{d x}\right)_{(1, f(1))}=f^{\prime}(1)=\tan \frac{\pi}{6}=\frac{1}{\sqrt{3}} \Rightarrow f^{\prime}(1)=\frac{1}{\sqrt{3}} \\ & \left.\frac{d y}{d x}\right)_{(3, f(3))}=f^{\prime}(3)=\tan \frac{\pi}{4}=1 \Rightarrow f^{\prime}(3)=1 \\ & 27 \int_\limits1^3\left(\left(f^{\prime}(t)\right)^2+1\right) f^{\prime \prime}(t) d t=\alpha+\beta \sqrt{3} \\ & I=\int_\limits1^3\left(\left(f^{\prime}(t)\right)^2+1\right) f^{\prime \prime}(t) d t \\ & f^{\prime}(t)=z \Rightarrow f^{\prime \prime}(t) d t=d z \\ & z=f^{\prime}(3)=1 \\ & z=f^{\prime}(1)=\frac{1}{\sqrt{3}} \end{aligned}$$

$$\begin{aligned} & I=\int_\limits{1 / \sqrt{3}}^1\left(z^2+1\right) d z=\left(\frac{z^3}{3}+z\right)_{1 / \sqrt{3}}^1 \\ & =\left(\frac{1}{3}+1\right)-\left(\frac{1}{3} \cdot \frac{1}{3 \sqrt{3}}+\frac{1}{\sqrt{3}}\right) \\ & =\frac{4}{3}-\frac{10}{9 \sqrt{3}}=\frac{4}{3}-\frac{10}{27} \sqrt{3} \\ & \alpha+\beta \sqrt{3}=27\left(\frac{4}{3}-\frac{10}{27} \sqrt{3}\right)=36-10 \sqrt{3} \\ & \alpha=36, \beta=-10 \\ & \alpha+\beta=36-10=26 \end{aligned}$$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.questionText: $\int_{\sqrt{2}}^{2} f(x) dx$  is equal to ?
questions.solution:
To evaluate

$$I=\int_{\sqrt2}^{2}\bigl\lfloor x^2\bigr\rfloor\,dx,$$

we observe where $x^2$ crosses integer values in the interval $[\sqrt2,2]$.

Solve $x^2=2\;\Rightarrow\;x=\sqrt2$.

Solve $x^2=3\;\Rightarrow\;x=\sqrt3$.

Solve $x^2=4\;\Rightarrow\;x=2$.

Thus on:

$x\in[\sqrt2,\sqrt3)$, we have $2\le x^2<3\implies\lfloor x^2\rfloor=2$.

$x\in[\sqrt3,2)$, we have $3\le x^2<4\implies\lfloor x^2\rfloor=3$.

We split the integral accordingly:

$$ I=\int_{\sqrt2}^{\sqrt3}2\,dx\;+\;\int_{\sqrt3}^{2}3\,dx. $$

Compute each part:

$\displaystyle \int_{\sqrt2}^{\sqrt3}2\,dx = 2\bigl[\;x\;\bigr]_{x=\sqrt2}^{\sqrt3} = 2\bigl(\sqrt3-\sqrt2\bigr).$

$\displaystyle \int_{\sqrt3}^{2}3\,dx = 3\bigl[\;x\;\bigr]_{x=\sqrt3}^{2} = 3\bigl(2-\sqrt3\bigr).$

So

$ I = 2(\sqrt3-\sqrt2)\;+\;3(2-\sqrt3) =2\sqrt3-2\sqrt2+6-3\sqrt3 =6 -\sqrt3 -2\sqrt2. $

Answer: $\displaystyle 6-\sqrt3-2\sqrt2,$ which is Option A.
